pattern: Only the merged element's two new neighboring pairs ever change after a merge, so tracking the array as a doubly linked list with a lazily-validated min-heap of adjacent-pair sums and a running count of out-of-order neighbors turns the same fully deterministic leftmost-minimum-sum process into a near-linear simulation instead of a quadratic one.
hints: The same deterministic rule as the small version applies -- always merge the leftmost adjacent pair with the smallest sum -- but rescanning the whole array after every merge is too slow at this size; only the merged node's new neighbors ever change.

Model the array as a doubly linked list so a merge is an O(1) splice, and keep a min-heap of (pair sum, left index, right index) for every adjacent pair, re-validating a popped entry against the current linked-list state and discarding it if it's stale (its neighbors changed since it was pushed).

Maintain a running count of adjacent positions that are currently out of order; update it by only re-examining the (at most two) neighboring pairs touched by each merge, and stop as soon as that count reaches zero.

edge_inputs.description: a single element is trivially sorted
several adjacent pairs tie for the minimum sum, so the leftmost one must be chosen first
strictly decreasing values, forcing repeated merges from the right before the array can settle
negative numbers whose pair sums are more negative than any positive pairing, so merging starts from the negative cluster
a longer mixed sequence needing several rounds of merging
extreme magnitude values at both ends of the allowed range

stress.description: random arrays near the maximum length across the full value range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(100000, size))
    nums = [rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: a strictly decreasing sequence, a known worst-case pattern forcing a long merge chain
